content.content: src\shell\scripts\omp.zsh

1	export POSH_SHELL='zsh'
2	export POSH_SHELL_VERSION=$ZSH_VERSION
3	export POWERLINE_COMMAND='oh-my-posh'
4	export CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER=false
5	export ZLE_RPROMPT_INDENT=0
6	export OSTYPE=$OSTYPE
7	
8	# disable all known python virtual environment prompts
9	export VIRTUAL_ENV_DISABLE_PROMPT=1
10	export PYENV_VIRTUALENV_DISABLE_PROMPT=1
11	
12	_omp_executable=::OMP::
13	_omp_tooltip_command=''
14	
15	# zsh/datetime provides the epochtime array for native millisecond timestamps
16	zmodload zsh/datetime 2>/dev/null
17	
18	# switches to enable/disable features
19	_omp_cursor_positioning=0
20	_omp_ftcs_marks=0
21	
22	# streaming support variables
23	# Preserve the current fd value if the script is re-sourced mid-session; default to -1 when unset or empty.
24	_omp_stream_fd=${_omp_stream_fd:--1}
25	_omp_enable_streaming=0
26	_omp_primary_prompt=""
27	_omp_transient_prompt=""
28	_omp_streaming_supported=""
29	
30	# serve daemon variables
31	# A persistent `oh-my-posh serve` process renders prompts on request, replacing
32	# a process spawn per prompt with an in-memory render. Preserve the fds when
33	# the script is re-sourced mid-session so the running daemon is reused.
34	_omp_serve_fd_in=${_omp_serve_fd_in:--1}
35	_omp_serve_fd_out=${_omp_serve_fd_out:--1}
36	_omp_serve_pid=${_omp_serve_pid:-0}
37	_omp_serve_cycle=0
38	_omp_serve_failures=0
39	
40	# set secondary prompt
41	_omp_secondary_prompt=$($_omp_executable print secondary --shell=zsh)
42	
43	function _omp_set_cursor_position() {
44	  # not supported in Midnight Commander
45	  # see https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/3415
46	  if [[ $_omp_cursor_positioning == 0 ]] || [[ -v MC_SID ]]; then
47	    return
48	  fi
49	
50	  local oldstty=$(stty -g)
51	  stty raw -echo min 0
52	
53	  local pos
54	  echo -en '\033[6n' >/dev/tty
55	  read -r -d R pos
56	  pos=${pos:2} # strip off the esc-[
57	  local parts=(${(s:;:)pos})
58	
59	  stty $oldstty
60	
61	  export POSH_CURSOR_LINE=${parts[1]}
62	  export POSH_CURSOR_COLUMN=${parts[2]}
63	}
64	
65	# template function for context loading
66	function set_poshcontext() {
67	  return
68	}
69	
70	# cleanup stream resources
71	function _omp_cleanup_stream() {
72	  # unregister handler first (prevents handler firing on closed fd)
73	  [[ $_omp_stream_fd -ge 0 ]] && zle -F $_omp_stream_fd 2>/dev/null
74	  # close fd — process gets SIGPIPE and terminates
75	  [[ $_omp_stream_fd -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_stream_fd}<&-" 2>/dev/null
76	  _omp_stream_fd=-1
77	}
78	
79	# start oh-my-posh stream process, block until first prompt arrives
80	function _omp_start_streaming() {
81	  # cleanup any stale streams
82	  _omp_cleanup_stream
83	
84	  # the transient prompt for this cycle streams in alongside the primary
85	  # prompt updates, invalidate the previous cycle's version
86	  _omp_transient_prompt=""
87	
88	  # build command with all context
89	  local -a stream_cmd=(
90	    "$_omp_executable" stream
91	    --save-cache
92	    --shell=zsh
93	    --shell-version="$ZSH_VERSION"
94	    --status=$_omp_status
95	    --pipestatus="${_omp_pipestatus[*]}"
96	    --no-status=$_omp_no_status
97	    --execution-time=$_omp_execution_time
98	    --job-count=$_omp_job_count
99	    --stack-count=$_omp_stack_count
100	    --terminal-width="${COLUMNS:-0}"
101	  )
102	
103	  # start process substitution — no PID tracking needed
104	  # closing fd sends SIGPIPE which terminates oh-my-posh
105	  # redirect stream process stdin to prevent it from interfering with command output
106	  exec {_omp_stream_fd}< <(exec "${stream_cmd[@]}" </dev/null 2>/dev/null)
107	
108	  if [[ $_omp_stream_fd -lt 0 ]]; then
109	    return 1
110	  fi
111	
112	  # block until first prompt arrives (mirrors PowerShell's polling loop)
113	  IFS= read -r -u $_omp_stream_fd -d $'\0' _omp_primary_prompt
114	  if [[ $? -ne 0 || -z "$_omp_primary_prompt" ]]; then
115	    _omp_cleanup_stream
116	    return 1
117	  fi
118	
119	  PS1="$_omp_primary_prompt"
120	
121	  return 0
122	}
123	
124	# async handler: called when data available on fd (reads single value)
125	function _omp_async_handler() {
126	  local fd=$1
127	  local record
128	
129	  # read single null-delimited record (stream emits one value per \0)
130	  IFS= read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' record
131	  if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
132	    if [[ -z "$record" ]]; then
133	      # EOF — only clean up if this is still the active stream fd.
134	      # A stale handler from a previous prompt cycle must not close the current stream.
135	      [[ $_omp_stream_fd -eq $fd ]] && _omp_cleanup_stream
136	      return 0
137	    fi
138	  fi
139	
140	  # a record prefixed with U+001E carries the transient prompt: cache it for
141	  # the line-init widget so rendering the transient prompt needs no CLI call
142	  if [[ $record == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
143	    _omp_transient_prompt=${record#$'\x1e'}
144	    return 0
145	  fi
146	
147	  _omp_primary_prompt=$record
148	  PS1="$_omp_primary_prompt"
149	  zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null
150	
151	  return 0
152	}
153	
154	# === serve daemon ===
155	
156	function _omp_serve_stop() {
157	  # unregister the zle watcher before closing its fd
158	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -ge 0 ]] && zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out 2>/dev/null
159	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&-" 2>/dev/null
160	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&-" 2>/dev/null
161	  _omp_serve_fd_in=-1
162	  _omp_serve_fd_out=-1
163	}
164	
165	function _omp_serve_start() {
166	  _omp_serve_stop
167	
168	  # With job control active, an interactive zsh announces the coproc on the
169	  # terminal like any background job ("[1] 12345"). Disabling MONITOR for the
170	  # spawn suppresses the notice; as a side effect the daemon starts with
171	  # SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored (POSIX no-job-control semantics), so Ctrl+C at the
172	  # prompt cannot take it down.
173	  setopt localoptions no_monitor
174	
175	  # The daemon's stderr must never reach the terminal (a Go panic would
176	  # corrupt the display).
177	  coproc "$_omp_executable" serve --shell=zsh 2>/dev/null
178	  [[ $? -ne 0 ]] && return 1
179	  _omp_serve_pid=$!
180	
181	  # Duplicate both directions to session fds: the duplicates survive a later
182	  # `coproc` (ours or the user's) replacing the coproc slot. The stderr
183	  # redirect (suppressing "no coprocess" when the daemon failed to start) must
184	  # be scoped to a block: on a redirection-only `exec`, zsh applies every
185	  # listed redirection to the shell permanently, which would silence the
186	  # session's stderr for good.
187	  { exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&p {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&p } 2>/dev/null
188	  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 || $_omp_serve_fd_out -lt 0 ]]; then
189	    _omp_serve_stop
190	    return 1
191	  fi
192	
193	  # Keep the daemon out of the job table: it must not show up in `jobs` or
194	  # inflate the job-count segment. Lifetime is governed by the fds - closing
195	  # them (or the shell dying) EOFs the daemon's stdin and it exits.
196	  disown %+ 2>/dev/null
197	
198	  # One session-long watcher delivers async records (segment updates and the
199	  # transient refresh) while zle is active. It never races the synchronous
200	  # read in _omp_serve_render: zle - and therefore this watcher - is not
201	  # active while precmd runs.
202	  zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out _omp_serve_async_handler 2>/dev/null
203	
204	  return 0
205	}
206	
207	function _omp_serve_escape() {
208	  # JSON string escaping using native parameter expansion; the result is
209	  # returned in REPLY. Any control characters left after the named escapes
210	  # are stripped - JSON forbids them raw.
211	  local s=$1
212	  s=${s//\\/\\\\}
213	  s=${s//\"/\\\"}
214	  s=${s//$'\n'/\\n}
215	  s=${s//$'\r'/\\r}
216	  s=${s//$'\t'/\\t}
217	  REPLY=${s//[[:cntrl:]]/}
218	}
219	
220	function _omp_serve_request() {
221	  # A write to a dead daemon's pipe raises SIGPIPE, which kills a
222	  # non-interactive shell outright - ignore it for the duration of this
223	  # function (localtraps restores the user's disposition on return) so the
224	  # write degrades into the error path instead. The pid pre-check makes the
225	  # common case cheap; the trap covers the race.
226	  setopt localoptions localtraps
227	  trap '' PIPE
228	
229	  # never pass a possibly-zero pid to kill: `kill -0 0` signals the caller's
230	  # own process group and always succeeds
231	  [[ $_omp_serve_pid -gt 0 ]] || return 1
232	  kill -0 $_omp_serve_pid 2>/dev/null || return 1
233	
234	  (( _omp_serve_cycle++ ))
235	  _omp_transient_prompt=""
236	
237	  local name env_json
238	  _omp_serve_escape "$PATH"
239	  env_json="\"PATH\":\"$REPLY\""
240	
241	  # Forward every exported POSH_* variable plus the virtual-env markers; the
242	  # daemon's environment is otherwise frozen at its start.
243	  for name in ${(k)parameters[(I)POSH_*]}; do
244	    [[ ${parameters[$name]} == *export* ]] || continue
245	    _omp_serve_escape "${(P)name}"
246	    env_json+=",\"$name\":\"$REPLY\""
247	  done
248	
249	  for name in VIRTUAL_ENV CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER; do
250	    [[ -v $name ]] || continue
251	    _omp_serve_escape "${(P)name}"
252	    env_json+=",\"$name\":\"$REPLY\""
253	  done
254	
255	  _omp_serve_escape "$PWD"
256	
257	  local json='{"command":"render"'
258	  json+=",\"id\":$_omp_serve_cycle"
259	  json+=',"shell":"zsh"'
260	  json+=",\"shell-version\":\"$ZSH_VERSION\""
261	  json+=",\"status\":$_omp_status"
262	  json+=",\"pipestatus\":\"${_omp_pipestatus[*]}\""
263	  json+=",\"no-status\":$_omp_no_status"
264	  json+=",\"execution-time\":$_omp_execution_time"
265	  json+=",\"stack-count\":$_omp_stack_count"
266	  json+=",\"terminal-width\":${COLUMNS:-0}"
267	  json+=",\"job-count\":$_omp_job_count"
268	  json+=",\"pwd\":\"$REPLY\""
269	  json+=",\"env\":{$env_json}"
270	  json+='}'
271	
272	  print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- "$json" 2>/dev/null
273	}
274	
275	# Renders the primary prompt through the daemon. Returns nonzero on failure,
276	# in which case the caller falls back to the per-prompt stream. The transient
277	# prompt records are cached into $_omp_transient_prompt, either here or by the
278	# async watcher once zle is active.
279	function _omp_serve_render() {
280	  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 ]] && ! _omp_serve_start; then
281	    (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
282	    return 1
283	  fi
284	
285	  if ! _omp_serve_request; then
286	    # The daemon died since the last prompt - restart it once.
287	    if ! _omp_serve_start || ! _omp_serve_request; then
288	      (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
289	      _omp_serve_stop
290	      return 1
291	    fi
292	  fi
293	
294	  # Block until this cycle's first primary record; async updates and the
295	  # transient refresh arrive later through the zle watcher. Stale records
296	  # from an aborted cycle are discarded by the id check.
297	  local record id payload
298	  while true; do
299	    if ! IFS= read -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_out -d $'\0' -t 2 record; then
300	      (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
301	      _omp_serve_stop
302	      return 1
303	    fi
304	
305	    id=${record%%$'\x1f'*}
306	    [[ $id == $_omp_serve_cycle ]] || continue
307	    payload=${record#*$'\x1f'}
308	
309	    if [[ $payload == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
310	      _omp_transient_prompt=${payload#$'\x1e'}
311	      continue
312	    fi
313	
314	    _omp_primary_prompt=$payload
315	    PS1=$payload
316	    return 0
317	  done
318	}
319	
320	# async watcher: like _omp_async_handler, but for the daemon's id-prefixed
321	# records
322	function _omp_serve_async_handler() {
323	  local fd=$1
324	  local record
325	
326	  IFS= read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' record 2>/dev/null
327	  if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
328	    if [[ -z "$record" ]]; then
329	      # EOF - the daemon died. Only tear down if this is still the active fd;
330	      # a stale watcher must not close the current daemon's pipe.
331	      if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -eq $fd ]]; then
332	        _omp_serve_stop
333	      else
334	        zle -F $fd 2>/dev/null
335	      fi
336	      return 0
337	    fi
338	  fi
339	
340	  local id=${record%%$'\x1f'*}
341	  [[ $id == $_omp_serve_cycle ]] || return 0
342	  local payload=${record#*$'\x1f'}
343	
344	  if [[ $payload == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
345	    _omp_transient_prompt=${payload#$'\x1e'}
346	    return 0
347	  fi
348	
349	  _omp_primary_prompt=$payload
350	  PS1=$payload
351	  zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null
352	
353	  return 0
354	}
355	
356	function _omp_serve_abort() {
357	  setopt localoptions localtraps
358	  trap '' PIPE
359	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- '{"command":"abort"}' 2>/dev/null
360	}
361	
362	function _omp_serve_quit() {
363	  setopt localoptions localtraps
364	  trap '' PIPE
365	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- '{"command":"quit"}' 2>/dev/null
366	  _omp_serve_stop
367	}
368	
369	# shell exit handler
370	function _omp_exit_handler() {
371	  _omp_serve_quit
372	  _omp_cleanup_stream
373	}
374	
375	# register exit handler
376	zshexit_functions+=(_omp_exit_handler)
377	
378	# sets _omp_millis instead of printing to avoid forking a subshell
379	function _omp_milliseconds() {
380	  if (( ${+epochtime} )); then
381	    # copy first: every expansion of epochtime reads the clock again,
382	    # so referencing it twice in one expression can straddle a second boundary
383	    local -a now=("${epochtime[@]}")
384	    _omp_millis=$((now[1] * 1000 + now[2] / 1000000))
385	    return
386	  fi
387	
388	  # zsh/datetime is unavailable
389	  _omp_millis=$($_omp_executable get millis)
390	}
391	
392	function _omp_preexec() {
393	  if [[ $_omp_ftcs_marks == 1 ]]; then
394	    printf '\033]133;C\007'
395	  fi
396	
397	  _omp_milliseconds
398	  _omp_start_time=$_omp_millis
399	}
400	
401	function _omp_precmd() {
402	  _omp_status=$?
403	  _omp_pipestatus=(${pipestatus[@]})
404	  _omp_job_count=${#jobstates}
405	  _omp_stack_count=${#dirstack[@]}
406	  _omp_execution_time=-1
407	  _omp_no_status=true
408	  _omp_tooltip_command=''
409	
410	  if [[ -n $_omp_start_time ]]; then
411	    _omp_milliseconds
412	    _omp_execution_time=$(($_omp_millis - $_omp_start_time))
413	    _omp_no_status=false
414	  fi
415	
416	  if [[ ${_omp_pipestatus[-1]} != "$_omp_status" ]]; then
417	    _omp_pipestatus=("$_omp_status")
418	  fi
419	
420	  set_poshcontext
421	  _omp_set_cursor_position
422	
423	  # We do this to avoid unexpected expansions in a prompt string.
424	  unsetopt PROMPT_SUBST
425	  unsetopt PROMPT_BANG
426	
427	  # Ensure that escape sequences work in a prompt string.
428	  setopt PROMPT_PERCENT
429	
430	  PS2=$_omp_secondary_prompt
431	
432	  # === STREAMING PATH ===
433	  if [[ $_omp_enable_streaming -eq 1 ]]; then
434	    # set RPROMPT synchronously (records only carry the primary prompt)
435	    RPROMPT=$(_omp_get_prompt right)
436	
437	    # serve daemon: persistent process, no spawn per prompt. After three
438	    # failures the daemon is left alone for the session and every prompt
439	    # takes the per-prompt stream below instead.
440	    if [[ $_omp_serve_failures -lt 3 ]] && _omp_serve_render; then
441	      unset _omp_start_time
442	      return 0
443	    fi
444	
445	    # per-prompt stream (also the serve fallback; blocks until first prompt arrives)
446	    if _omp_start_streaming; then
447	      # PS1 already set by _omp_start_streaming (blocking first read)
448	      zle -F $_omp_stream_fd _omp_async_handler
449	      unset _omp_start_time
450	      return 0
451	    fi
452	    # fall through to sync
453	  fi
454	
455	  # === FALLBACK PATH ===
456	  eval "$(_omp_get_prompt primary --eval)"
457	
458	  unset _omp_start_time
459	}
460	
461	# add hook functions
462	autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
463	add-zsh-hook precmd _omp_precmd
464	add-zsh-hook preexec _omp_preexec
465	
466	# Prevent incorrect behaviors when the initialization is executed twice in current session.
467	function _omp_cleanup() {
468	  local omp_widgets=(
469	    self-insert
470	    zle-line-init
471	    backward-delete-char
472	  )
473	  local widget
474	  for widget in "${omp_widgets[@]}"; do
475	    if [[ ${widgets[._omp_original::$widget]} ]]; then
476	      # Restore the original widget.
477	      zle -A ._omp_original::$widget $widget
478	    elif [[ ${widgets[$widget]} = user:_omp_* ]]; then
479	      # Delete the OMP-defined widget.
480	      zle -D $widget
481	    fi
482	  done
483	}
484	_omp_cleanup
485	unset -f _omp_cleanup
486	
487	function _omp_get_prompt() {
488	  local type=$1
489	  local args=("${@[2,-1]}")
490	  $_omp_executable print $type \
491	    --save-cache \
492	    --shell=zsh \
493	    --shell-version=$ZSH_VERSION \
494	    --status=$_omp_status \
495	    --pipestatus="${_omp_pipestatus[*]}" \
496	    --no-status=$_omp_no_status \
497	    --execution-time=$_omp_execution_time \
498	    --job-count=$_omp_job_count \
499	    --stack-count=$_omp_stack_count \
500	    --terminal-width="${COLUMNS-0}" \
501	    ${args[@]}
502	}
503	
504	function _omp_render_tooltip() {
505	  if [[ $KEYS != ' ' ]]; then
506	    return
507	  fi
508	
509	  setopt local_options no_shwordsplit
510	
511	  # Get the first word of command line as tip.
512	  local tooltip_command=${${(MS)BUFFER##[[:graph:]]*}%%[[:space:]]*}
513	
514	  # Ignore an empty/repeated tooltip command.
515	  if [[ -z $tooltip_command ]] || [[ $tooltip_command = "$_omp_tooltip_command" ]]; then
516	    return
517	  fi
518	
519	  _omp_tooltip_command="$tooltip_command"
520	  local tooltip=$(_omp_get_prompt tooltip --command="$tooltip_command")
521	  if [[ -z $tooltip ]]; then
522	    return
523	  fi
524	
525	  RPROMPT=$tooltip
526	  zle .reset-prompt
527	}
528	
529	function _omp_restore_rprompt() {
530	  if [[ -z $_omp_tooltip_command ]]; then
531	    return
532	  fi
533	
534	  setopt local_options no_shwordsplit
535	
536	  local current_command=${${(MS)BUFFER##[[:graph:]]*}%%[[:space:]]*}
537	
538	  if [[ $current_command = "$_omp_tooltip_command" ]]; then
539	    return
540	  fi
541	
542	  _omp_tooltip_command="$current_command"
543	  RPROMPT=$(_omp_get_prompt tooltip --command="$current_command")
544	  zle .reset-prompt
545	}
546	
547	function _omp_zle-line-init() {
548	  [[ $CONTEXT == start ]] || return 0
549	
550	  # Start regular line editor.
551	  (( $+zle_bracketed_paste )) && print -r -n - $zle_bracketed_paste[1]
552	  zle .recursive-edit
553	  local -i ret=$?
554	  (( $+zle_bracketed_paste )) && print -r -n - $zle_bracketed_paste[2]
555	
556	  # We need this workaround because when the `filler` is set,
557	  # there will be a redundant blank line below the transient prompt if the input is empty.
558	  local terminal_width_option
559	  if [[ -z $BUFFER ]]; then
560	    terminal_width_option="--terminal-width=$((${COLUMNS-0} - 1))"
561	  fi
562	
563	  # stop prompt updates before the transient prompt renders so no handler
564	  # overwrites it: kill the per-prompt stream, tell the daemon to abort its
565	  # in-flight cycle (the daemon itself lives on)
566	  _omp_cleanup_stream
567	  _omp_serve_abort
568	
569	  if ((ret)); then
570	    # interrupted (e.g. Ctrl-C): a pre-rendered transient prompt was built
571	    # before the interrupt and can't carry .Interrupted, so always re-render
572	    # through the CLI with the flag set
573	    eval "$(_omp_get_prompt transient --eval $terminal_width_option --interrupted)"
574	  elif [[ -n $_omp_transient_prompt ]]; then
575	    # rendered ahead of time by the streaming process (one column narrower,
576	    # mirroring the empty-buffer workaround above), saves a CLI call
577	    PS1=$_omp_transient_prompt
578	    RPROMPT=''
579	  else
580	    eval "$(_omp_get_prompt transient --eval $terminal_width_option)"
581	  fi
582	  zle .reset-prompt
583	
584	  if ((ret)); then
585	    # TODO (fix): this is not equal to sending a SIGINT, since the status code ($?) is set to 1 instead of 130.
586	    zle .send-break
587	  fi
588	
589	  # Exit the shell if we receive EOT.
590	  if [[ $KEYS == $'\4' ]]; then
591	    exit
592	  fi
593	
594	  zle .accept-line
595	  return $ret
596	}
597	
598	# Helper function for calling a widget before the specified OMP function.
599	function _omp_call_widget() {
600	  # The name of the OMP function.
601	  local omp_func=$1
602	  # The remainder are the widget to call and potential arguments.
603	  shift
604	
605	  zle "$@" && shift 2 && $omp_func "$@"
606	}
607	
608	# Create a widget with the specified OMP function.
609	# An existing widget will be preserved and decorated with the function.
610	function _omp_create_widget() {
611	  # The name of the widget to create/decorate.
612	  local widget=$1
613	  # The name of the OMP function.
614	  local omp_func=$2
615	
616	  case ${widgets[$widget]:-''} in
617	  # Already decorated: do nothing.
618	  user:_omp_decorated_*) ;;
619	
620	  # Non-existent: just create it.
621	  '')
622	    zle -N $widget $omp_func
623	    ;;
624	
625	  # User-defined or builtin: backup and decorate it.
626	  *)
627	    # Back up the original widget. The leading dot in widget name is to work around bugs when used with zsh-syntax-highlighting in Zsh v5.8 or lower.
628	    zle -A $widget ._omp_original::$widget
629	    eval "_omp_decorated_${(q)widget}() { _omp_call_widget ${(q)omp_func} ._omp_original::${(q)widget} -- \"\$@\" }"
630	    zle -N $widget _omp_decorated_$widget
631	    ;;
632	  esac
633	}
634	
635	function enable_poshtooltips() {
636	  local widget=${$(bindkey ' '):2}
637	
638	  if [[ -z $widget ]]; then
639	    widget=self-insert
640	  fi
641	
642	  _omp_create_widget $widget _omp_render_tooltip
643	  _omp_create_widget backward-delete-char _omp_restore_rprompt
644	}
645	
646	# vi mode tracking — re-render the prompt whenever the active keymap changes
647	function _omp_render_vimode() {
648	  export POSH_VI_MODE=${KEYMAP:-main}
649	  eval "$(_omp_get_prompt primary --eval)"
650	  zle .reset-prompt
651	}
652	
653	function _omp_enable_vimode() {
654	  export POSH_VI_MODE=${POSH_VI_MODE:-main}
655	  _omp_create_widget zle-keymap-select _omp_render_vimode
656	}
657	
658	# legacy functions
659	function enable_poshtransientprompt() {}
660

file.content:
export POSH_SHELL='zsh'
export POSH_SHELL_VERSION=$ZSH_VERSION
export POWERLINE_COMMAND='oh-my-posh'
export CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER=false
export ZLE_RPROMPT_INDENT=0
export OSTYPE=$OSTYPE

# disable all known python virtual environment prompts
export VIRTUAL_ENV_DISABLE_PROMPT=1
export PYENV_VIRTUALENV_DISABLE_PROMPT=1

_omp_executable=::OMP::
_omp_tooltip_command=''

# zsh/datetime provides the epochtime array for native millisecond timestamps
zmodload zsh/datetime 2>/dev/null

# switches to enable/disable features
_omp_cursor_positioning=0
_omp_ftcs_marks=0

# streaming support variables
# Preserve the current fd value if the script is re-sourced mid-session; default to -1 when unset or empty.
_omp_stream_fd=${_omp_stream_fd:--1}
_omp_enable_streaming=0
_omp_primary_prompt=""
_omp_transient_prompt=""
_omp_streaming_supported=""

# serve daemon variables
# A persistent `oh-my-posh serve` process renders prompts on request, replacing
# a process spawn per prompt with an in-memory render. Preserve the fds when
# the script is re-sourced mid-session so the running daemon is reused.
_omp_serve_fd_in=${_omp_serve_fd_in:--1}
_omp_serve_fd_out=${_omp_serve_fd_out:--1}
_omp_serve_pid=${_omp_serve_pid:-0}
_omp_serve_cycle=0
_omp_serve_failures=0

# set secondary prompt
_omp_secondary_prompt=$($_omp_executable print secondary --shell=zsh)

function _omp_set_cursor_position() {
  # not supported in Midnight Commander
  # see https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/3415
  if [[ $_omp_cursor_positioning == 0 ]] || [[ -v MC_SID ]]; then
    return
  fi

  local oldstty=$(stty -g)
  stty raw -echo min 0

  local pos
  echo -en '\033[6n' >/dev/tty
  read -r -d R pos
  pos=${pos:2} # strip off the esc-[
  local parts=(${(s:;:)pos})

  stty $oldstty

  export POSH_CURSOR_LINE=${parts[1]}
  export POSH_CURSOR_COLUMN=${parts[2]}
}

# template function for context loading
function set_poshcontext() {
  return
}

# cleanup stream resources
function _omp_cleanup_stream() {
  # unregister handler first (prevents handler firing on closed fd)
  [[ $_omp_stream_fd -ge 0 ]] && zle -F $_omp_stream_fd 2>/dev/null
  # close fd — process gets SIGPIPE and terminates
  [[ $_omp_stream_fd -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_stream_fd}<&-" 2>/dev/null
  _omp_stream_fd=-1
}

# start oh-my-posh stream process, block until first prompt arrives
function _omp_start_streaming() {
  # cleanup any stale streams
  _omp_cleanup_stream

  # the transient prompt for this cycle streams in alongside the primary
  # prompt updates, invalidate the previous cycle's version
  _omp_transient_prompt=""

  # build command with all context
  local -a stream_cmd=(
    "$_omp_executable" stream
    --save-cache
    --shell=zsh
    --shell-version="$ZSH_VERSION"
    --status=$_omp_status
    --pipestatus="${_omp_pipestatus[*]}"
    --no-status=$_omp_no_status
    --execution-time=$_omp_execution_time
    --job-count=$_omp_job_count
    --stack-count=$_omp_stack_count
    --terminal-width="${COLUMNS:-0}"
  )

  # start process substitution — no PID tracking needed
  # closing fd sends SIGPIPE which terminates oh-my-posh
  # redirect stream process stdin to prevent it from interfering with command output
  exec {_omp_stream_fd}< <(exec "${stream_cmd[@]}" </dev/null 2>/dev/null)

  if [[ $_omp_stream_fd -lt 0 ]]; then
    return 1
  fi

  # block until first prompt arrives (mirrors PowerShell's polling loop)
  IFS= read -r -u $_omp_stream_fd -d $'\0' _omp_primary_prompt
  if [[ $? -ne 0 || -z "$_omp_primary_prompt" ]]; then
    _omp_cleanup_stream
    return 1
  fi

  PS1="$_omp_primary_prompt"

  return 0
}

# async handler: called when data available on fd (reads single value)
function _omp_async_handler() {
  local fd=$1
  local record

  # read single null-delimited record (stream emits one value per \0)
  IFS= read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' record
  if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
    if [[ -z "$record" ]]; then
      # EOF — only clean up if this is still the active stream fd.
      # A stale handler from a previous prompt cycle must not close the current stream.
      [[ $_omp_stream_fd -eq $fd ]] && _omp_cleanup_stream
      return 0
    fi
  fi

  # a record prefixed with U+001E carries the transient prompt: cache it for
  # the line-init widget so rendering the transient prompt needs no CLI call
  if [[ $record == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
    _omp_transient_prompt=${record#$'\x1e'}
    return 0
  fi

  _omp_primary_prompt=$record
  PS1="$_omp_primary_prompt"
  zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null

  return 0
}

# === serve daemon ===

function _omp_serve_stop() {
  # unregister the zle watcher before closing its fd
  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -ge 0 ]] && zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out 2>/dev/null
  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&-" 2>/dev/null
  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&-" 2>/dev/null
  _omp_serve_fd_in=-1
  _omp_serve_fd_out=-1
}

function _omp_serve_start() {
  _omp_serve_stop

  # With job control active, an interactive zsh announces the coproc on the
  # terminal like any background job ("[1] 12345"). Disabling MONITOR for the
  # spawn suppresses the notice; as a side effect the daemon starts with
  # SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored (POSIX no-job-control semantics), so Ctrl+C at the
  # prompt cannot take it down.
  setopt localoptions no_monitor

  # The daemon's stderr must never reach the terminal (a Go panic would
  # corrupt the display).
  coproc "$_omp_executable" serve --shell=zsh 2>/dev/null
  [[ $? -ne 0 ]] && return 1
  _omp_serve_pid=$!

  # Duplicate both directions to session fds: the duplicates survive a later
  # `coproc` (ours or the user's) replacing the coproc slot. The stderr
  # redirect (suppressing "no coprocess" when the daemon failed to start) must
  # be scoped to a block: on a redirection-only `exec`, zsh applies every
  # listed redirection to the shell permanently, which would silence the
  # session's stderr for good.
  { exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&p {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&p } 2>/dev/null
  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 || $_omp_serve_fd_out -lt 0 ]]; then
    _omp_serve_stop
    return 1
  fi

  # Keep the daemon out of the job table: it must not show up in `jobs` or
  # inflate the job-count segment. Lifetime is governed by the fds - closing
  # them (or the shell dying) EOFs the daemon's stdin and it exits.
  disown %+ 2>/dev/null

  # One session-long watcher delivers async records (segment updates and the
  # transient refresh) while zle is active. It never races the synchronous
  # read in _omp_serve_render: zle - and therefore this watcher - is not
  # active while precmd runs.
  zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out _omp_serve_async_handler 2>/dev/null

  return 0
}

function _omp_serve_escape() {
  # JSON string escaping using native parameter expansion; the result is
  # returned in REPLY. Any control characters left after the named escapes
  # are stripped - JSON forbids them raw.
  local s=$1
  s=${s//\\/\\\\}
  s=${s//\"/\\\"}
  s=${s//$'\n'/\\n}
  s=${s//$'\r'/\\r}
  s=${s//$'\t'/\\t}
  REPLY=${s//[[:cntrl:]]/}
}

function _omp_serve_request() {
  # A write to a dead daemon's pipe raises SIGPIPE, which kills a
  # non-interactive shell outright - ignore it for the duration of this
  # function (localtraps restores the user's disposition on return) so the
  # write degrades into the error path instead. The pid pre-check makes the
  # common case cheap; the trap covers the race.
  setopt localoptions localtraps
  trap '' PIPE

  # never pass a possibly-zero pid to kill: `kill -0 0` signals the caller's
  # own process group and always succeeds
  [[ $_omp_serve_pid -gt 0 ]] || return 1
  kill -0 $_omp_serve_pid 2>/dev/null || return 1

  (( _omp_serve_cycle++ ))
  _omp_transient_prompt=""

  local name env_json
  _omp_serve_escape "$PATH"
  env_json="\"PATH\":\"$REPLY\""

  # Forward every exported POSH_* variable plus the virtual-env markers; the
  # daemon's environment is otherwise frozen at its start.
  for name in ${(k)parameters[(I)POSH_*]}; do
    [[ ${parameters[$name]} == *export* ]] || continue
    _omp_serve_escape "${(P)name}"
    env_json+=",\"$name\":\"$REPLY\""
  done

  for name in VIRTUAL_ENV CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER; do
    [[ -v $name ]] || continue
    _omp_serve_escape "${(P)name}"
    env_json+=",\"$name\":\"$REPLY\""
  done

  _omp_serve_escape "$PWD"

  local json='{"command":"render"'
  json+=",\"id\":$_omp_serve_cycle"
  json+=',"shell":"zsh"'
  json+=",\"shell-version\":\"$ZSH_VERSION\""
  json+=",\"status\":$_omp_status"
  json+=",\"pipestatus\":\"${_omp_pipestatus[*]}\""
  json+=",\"no-status\":$_omp_no_status"
  json+=",\"execution-time\":$_omp_execution_time"
  json+=",\"stack-count\":$_omp_stack_count"
  json+=",\"terminal-width\":${COLUMNS:-0}"
  json+=",\"job-count\":$_omp_job_count"
  json+=",\"pwd\":\"$REPLY\""
  json+=",\"env\":{$env_json}"
  json+='}'

  print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- "$json" 2>/dev/null
}

# Renders the primary prompt through the daemon. Returns nonzero on failure,
# in which case the caller falls back to the per-prompt stream. The transient
# prompt records are cached into $_omp_transient_prompt, either here or by the
# async watcher once zle is active.
function _omp_serve_render() {
  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 ]] && ! _omp_serve_start; then
    (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
    return 1
  fi

  if ! _omp_serve_request; then
    # The daemon died since the last prompt - restart it once.
    if ! _omp_serve_start || ! _omp_serve_request; then
      (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
      _omp_serve_stop
      return 1
    fi
  fi

  # Block until this cycle's first primary record; async updates and the
  # transient refresh arrive later through the zle watcher. Stale records
  # from an aborted cycle are discarded by the id check.
  local record id payload
  while true; do
    if ! IFS= read -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_out -d $'\0' -t 2 record; then
      (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
      _omp_serve_stop
      return 1
    fi

    id=${record%%$'\x1f'*}
    [[ $id == $_omp_serve_cycle ]] || continue
    payload=${record#*$'\x1f'}

    if [[ $payload == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
      _omp_transient_prompt=${payload#$'\x1e'}
      continue
    fi

    _omp_primary_prompt=$payload
    PS1=$payload
    return 0
  done
}

# async watcher: like _omp_async_handler, but for the daemon's id-prefixed
# records
function _omp_serve_async_handler() {
  local fd=$1
  local record

  IFS= read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' record 2>/dev/null
  if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
    if [[ -z "$record" ]]; then
      # EOF - the daemon died. Only tear down if this is still the active fd;
      # a stale watcher must not close the current daemon's pipe.
      if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -eq $fd ]]; then
        _omp_serve_stop
      else
        zle -F $fd 2>/dev/null
      fi
      return 0
    fi
  fi

  local id=${record%%$'\x1f'*}
  [[ $id == $_omp_serve_cycle ]] || return 0
  local payload=${record#*$'\x1f'}

  if [[ $payload == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
    _omp_transient_prompt=${payload#$'\x1e'}
    return 0
  fi

  _omp_primary_prompt=$payload
  PS1=$payload
  zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null

  return 0
}

function _omp_serve_abort() {
  setopt localoptions localtraps
  trap '' PIPE
  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- '{"command":"abort"}' 2>/dev/null
}

function _omp_serve_quit() {
  setopt localoptions localtraps
  trap '' PIPE
  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- '{"command":"quit"}' 2>/dev/null
  _omp_serve_stop
}

# shell exit handler
function _omp_exit_handler() {
  _omp_serve_quit
  _omp_cleanup_stream
}

# register exit handler
zshexit_functions+=(_omp_exit_handler)

# sets _omp_millis instead of printing to avoid forking a subshell
function _omp_milliseconds() {
  if (( ${+epochtime} )); then
    # copy first: every expansion of epochtime reads the clock again,
    # so referencing it twice in one expression can straddle a second boundary
    local -a now=("${epochtime[@]}")
    _omp_millis=$((now[1] * 1000 + now[2] / 1000000))
    return
  fi

  # zsh/datetime is unavailable
  _omp_millis=$($_omp_executable get millis)
}

function _omp_preexec() {
  if [[ $_omp_ftcs_marks == 1 ]]; then
    printf '\033]133;C\007'
  fi

  _omp_milliseconds
  _omp_start_time=$_omp_millis
}

function _omp_precmd() {
  _omp_status=$?
  _omp_pipestatus=(${pipestatus[@]})
  _omp_job_count=${#jobstates}
  _omp_stack_count=${#dirstack[@]}
  _omp_execution_time=-1
  _omp_no_status=true
  _omp_tooltip_command=''

  if [[ -n $_omp_start_time ]]; then
    _omp_milliseconds
    _omp_execution_time=$(($_omp_millis - $_omp_start_time))
    _omp_no_status=false
  fi

  if [[ ${_omp_pipestatus[-1]} != "$_omp_status" ]]; then
    _omp_pipestatus=("$_omp_status")
  fi

  set_poshcontext
  _omp_set_cursor_position

  # We do this to avoid unexpected expansions in a prompt string.
  unsetopt PROMPT_SUBST
  unsetopt PROMPT_BANG

  # Ensure that escape sequences work in a prompt string.
  setopt PROMPT_PERCENT

  PS2=$_omp_secondary_prompt

  # === STREAMING PATH ===
  if [[ $_omp_enable_streaming -eq 1 ]]; then
    # set RPROMPT synchronously (records only carry the primary prompt)
    RPROMPT=$(_omp_get_prompt right)

    # serve daemon: persistent process, no spawn per prompt. After three
    # failures the daemon is left alone for the session and every prompt
    # takes the per-prompt stream below instead.
    if [[ $_omp_serve_failures -lt 3 ]] && _omp_serve_render; then
      unset _omp_start_time
      return 0
    fi

    # per-prompt stream (also the serve fallback; blocks until first prompt arrives)
    if _omp_start_streaming; then
      # PS1 already set by _omp_start_streaming (blocking first read)
      zle -F $_omp_stream_fd _omp_async_handler
      unset _omp_start_time
      return 0
    fi
    # fall through to sync
  fi

  # === FALLBACK PATH ===
  eval "$(_omp_get_prompt primary --eval)"

  unset _omp_start_time
}

# add hook functions
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
add-zsh-hook precmd _omp_precmd
add-zsh-hook preexec _omp_preexec

# Prevent incorrect behaviors when the initialization is executed twice in current session.
function _omp_cleanup() {
  local omp_widgets=(
    self-insert
    zle-line-init
    backward-delete-char
  )
  local widget
  for widget in "${omp_widgets[@]}"; do
    if [[ ${widgets[._omp_original::$widget]} ]]; then
      # Restore the original widget.
      zle -A ._omp_original::$widget $widget
    elif [[ ${widgets[$widget]} = user:_omp_* ]]; then
      # Delete the OMP-defined widget.
      zle -D $widget
    fi
  done
}
_omp_cleanup
unset -f _omp_cleanup

function _omp_get_prompt() {
  local type=$1
  local args=("${@[2,-1]}")
  $_omp_executable print $type \
    --save-cache \
    --shell=zsh \
    --shell-version=$ZSH_VERSION \
    --status=$_omp_status \
    --pipestatus="${_omp_pipestatus[*]}" \
    --no-status=$_omp_no_status \
    --execution-time=$_omp_execution_time \
    --job-count=$_omp_job_count \
    --stack-count=$_omp_stack_count \
    --terminal-width="${COLUMNS-0}" \
    ${args[@]}
}

function _omp_render_tooltip() {
  if [[ $KEYS != ' ' ]]; then
    return
  fi

  setopt local_options no_shwordsplit

  # Get the first word of command line as tip.
  local tooltip_command=${${(MS)BUFFER##[[:graph:]]*}%%[[:space:]]*}

  # Ignore an empty/repeated tooltip command.
  if [[ -z $tooltip_command ]] || [[ $tooltip_command = "$_omp_tooltip_command" ]]; then
    return
  fi

  _omp_tooltip_command="$tooltip_command"
  local tooltip=$(_omp_get_prompt tooltip --command="$tooltip_command")
  if [[ -z $tooltip ]]; then
    return
  fi

  RPROMPT=$tooltip
  zle .reset-prompt
}

function _omp_restore_rprompt() {
  if [[ -z $_omp_tooltip_command ]]; then
    return
  fi

  setopt local_options no_shwordsplit

  local current_command=${${(MS)BUFFER##[[:graph:]]*}%%[[:space:]]*}

  if [[ $current_command = "$_omp_tooltip_command" ]]; then
    return
  fi

  _omp_tooltip_command="$current_command"
  RPROMPT=$(_omp_get_prompt tooltip --command="$current_command")
  zle .reset-prompt
}

function _omp_zle-line-init() {
  [[ $CONTEXT == start ]] || return 0

  # Start regular line editor.
  (( $+zle_bracketed_paste )) && print -r -n - $zle_bracketed_paste[1]
  zle .recursive-edit
  local -i ret=$?
  (( $+zle_bracketed_paste )) && print -r -n - $zle_bracketed_paste[2]

  # We need this workaround because when the `filler` is set,
  # there will be a redundant blank line below the transient prompt if the input is empty.
  local terminal_width_option
  if [[ -z $BUFFER ]]; then
    terminal_width_option="--terminal-width=$((${COLUMNS-0} - 1))"
  fi

  # stop prompt updates before the transient prompt renders so no handler
  # overwrites it: kill the per-prompt stream, tell the daemon to abort its
  # in-flight cycle (the daemon itself lives on)
  _omp_cleanup_stream
  _omp_serve_abort

  if ((ret)); then
    # interrupted (e.g. Ctrl-C): a pre-rendered transient prompt was built
    # before the interrupt and can't carry .Interrupted, so always re-render
    # through the CLI with the flag set
    eval "$(_omp_get_prompt transient --eval $terminal_width_option --interrupted)"
  elif [[ -n $_omp_transient_prompt ]]; then
    # rendered ahead of time by the streaming process (one column narrower,
    # mirroring the empty-buffer workaround above), saves a CLI call
    PS1=$_omp_transient_prompt
    RPROMPT=''
  else
    eval "$(_omp_get_prompt transient --eval $terminal_width_option)"
  fi
  zle .reset-prompt

  if ((ret)); then
    # TODO (fix): this is not equal to sending a SIGINT, since the status code ($?) is set to 1 instead of 130.
    zle .send-break
  fi

  # Exit the shell if we receive EOT.
  if [[ $KEYS == $'\4' ]]; then
    exit
  fi

  zle .accept-line
  return $ret
}

# Helper function for calling a widget before the specified OMP function.
function _omp_call_widget() {
  # The name of the OMP function.
  local omp_func=$1
  # The remainder are the widget to call and potential arguments.
  shift

  zle "$@" && shift 2 && $omp_func "$@"
}

# Create a widget with the specified OMP function.
# An existing widget will be preserved and decorated with the function.
function _omp_create_widget() {
  # The name of the widget to create/decorate.
  local widget=$1
  # The name of the OMP function.
  local omp_func=$2

  case ${widgets[$widget]:-''} in
  # Already decorated: do nothing.
  user:_omp_decorated_*) ;;

  # Non-existent: just create it.
  '')
    zle -N $widget $omp_func
    ;;

  # User-defined or builtin: backup and decorate it.
  *)
    # Back up the original widget. The leading dot in widget name is to work around bugs when used with zsh-syntax-highlighting in Zsh v5.8 or lower.
    zle -A $widget ._omp_original::$widget
    eval "_omp_decorated_${(q)widget}() { _omp_call_widget ${(q)omp_func} ._omp_original::${(q)widget} -- \"\$@\" }"
    zle -N $widget _omp_decorated_$widget
    ;;
  esac
}

function enable_poshtooltips() {
  local widget=${$(bindkey ' '):2}

  if [[ -z $widget ]]; then
    widget=self-insert
  fi

  _omp_create_widget $widget _omp_render_tooltip
  _omp_create_widget backward-delete-char _omp_restore_rprompt
}

# vi mode tracking — re-render the prompt whenever the active keymap changes
function _omp_render_vimode() {
  export POSH_VI_MODE=${KEYMAP:-main}
  eval "$(_omp_get_prompt primary --eval)"
  zle .reset-prompt
}

function _omp_enable_vimode() {
  export POSH_VI_MODE=${POSH_VI_MODE:-main}
  _omp_create_widget zle-keymap-select _omp_render_vimode
}

# legacy functions
function enable_poshtransientprompt() {}

file.content:
package shell

import (
	_ "embed"
)

//go:embed scripts/omp.zsh
var zshInit string

func (f Features) Zsh() Code {
	switch f {
	case CursorPositioning:
		return unixCursorPositioning
	case Tooltips:
		return "enable_poshtooltips"
	case Transient:
		return "_omp_create_widget zle-line-init _omp_zle-line-init"
	case FTCSMarks:
		return unixFTCSMarks
	case Upgrade:
		return unixUpgrade
	case Notice:
		return unixNotice
	case Streaming:
		return "_omp_enable_streaming=1"
	case VIMode:
		return "_omp_enable_vimode"
	case PromptMark, RPrompt, PoshGit, Azure, LineError, Jobs, Async, KeyHandlers:
		fallthrough
	default:
		return ""
	}
}

10	Testing src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh serve/streaming end-to-end in WSL (verified 2026-07-09, zsh 5.9), extends [[fish-streaming-wsl-testing]]:
11	
12	- `zsh -i` under `wsl -e` hangs before the first prompt (no foreground pty; job-control stop). Don't drive interactive zsh; instead source the generated init in `zsh -f` and call `_omp_precmd` directly — that exercises the exact serve-daemon startup path.
13	- To test job-control behavior (MONITOR on, e.g. coproc job notices, issue 7660): allocate a pty with `wsl -e script -qec 'zsh -fic "..."' /dev/null` — interactive zsh works fine under `script` and enables MONITOR. For the full init flow under a pty, run the `script` invocation backgrounded with output to a file and poll for a done marker (verified 2026-07-10). From Git Bash, prefix `MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1` so `/mnt/c/...` args reach wsl.exe unmangled.
14	- zsh coproc facts (verified): interactive+MONITOR prints "[n] pid" at spawn (disown is too late); a `{ coproc ... } 2>/dev/null` block does NOT suppress it; `setopt localoptions no_monitor` does, and the child then inherits SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored (POSIX no-job-control), which Go preserves.
15	- Foreground `zsh -f script` that starts the serve coproc can also wedge under `timeout`/wsl-relay; run it as `zsh -f script > out 2> err &` and poll for a done-marker file instead. This pattern is reliable.
16	- zsh 5.9 quirk (real code bug, flagged as task): `read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' -t N` ignores `-t` entirely and blocks forever on a silent fd. Without `-d`, the timeout works. This is why _omp_serve_render's 2s failover never fires.
17	- zsh exec footgun that caused issue 7653: a redirection-only `exec` applies EVERY listed redirection permanently, so `exec {fd}<&p {fd}>&p 2>/dev/null` silences the session's stderr for good. Scope the stderr suppress with a brace block: `{ exec ... } 2>/dev/null` — the named-fd dups persist, fd 2 is restored.
18	- Differential testing: build fixed binary from working tree, control from HEAD via `git stash push -- <file>`, build, `git stash pop` (script is embedded at build time).

file.content:
---
name: zsh-streaming-wsl-testing
description: "How to functionally test omp.zsh streaming/serve paths in WSL — no-pty hangs, background-with-files pattern, zsh read -d -t timeout quirk"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 0208eda7-3cd0-486e-a7af-2f6e14d5708e
---

Testing src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh serve/streaming end-to-end in WSL (verified 2026-07-09, zsh 5.9), extends [[fish-streaming-wsl-testing]]:

- `zsh -i` under `wsl -e` hangs before the first prompt (no foreground pty; job-control stop). Don't drive interactive zsh; instead source the generated init in `zsh -f` and call `_omp_precmd` directly — that exercises the exact serve-daemon startup path.
- To test job-control behavior (MONITOR on, e.g. coproc job notices, issue 7660): allocate a pty with `wsl -e script -qec 'zsh -fic "..."' /dev/null` — interactive zsh works fine under `script` and enables MONITOR. For the full init flow under a pty, run the `script` invocation backgrounded with output to a file and poll for a done marker (verified 2026-07-10). From Git Bash, prefix `MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1` so `/mnt/c/...` args reach wsl.exe unmangled.
- zsh coproc facts (verified): interactive+MONITOR prints "[n] pid" at spawn (disown is too late); a `{ coproc ... } 2>/dev/null` block does NOT suppress it; `setopt localoptions no_monitor` does, and the child then inherits SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored (POSIX no-job-control), which Go preserves.
- Foreground `zsh -f script` that starts the serve coproc can also wedge under `timeout`/wsl-relay; run it as `zsh -f script > out 2> err &` and poll for a done-marker file instead. This pattern is reliable.
- zsh 5.9 quirk (real code bug, flagged as task): `read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' -t N` ignores `-t` entirely and blocks forever on a silent fd. Without `-d`, the timeout works. This is why _omp_serve_render's 2s failover never fires.
- zsh exec footgun that caused issue 7653: a redirection-only `exec` applies EVERY listed redirection permanently, so `exec {fd}<&p {fd}>&p 2>/dev/null` silences the session's stderr for good. Scope the stderr suppress with a brace block: `{ exec ... } 2>/dev/null` — the named-fd dups persist, fd 2 is restored.
- Differential testing: build fixed binary from working tree, control from HEAD via `git stash push -- <file>`, build, `git stash pop` (script is embedded at build time).

# Define widget function
function zvm_define_widget() {
  local widget=$1
  local func=$2 || $1
  local result=($(zle -l -L "${widget}"))

  # Check if existing the same name
  if [[ ${#result[@]} == 4 ]]; then
    local rawfunc=${result[4]}
    local wrapper="zvm_${widget}-wrapper"

    # To avoid double calling, we need to check if the raw function
    # has been called already in the custom widget function
    local rawcode=$(declare -f $func 2>/dev/null)
    local called=false
    [[ "$rawcode" == *"\$rawfunc"* ]] && { called=true }

    eval "$wrapper() { zvm_widget_wrapper $rawfunc $func $called \"\$@\" }"
    func=$wrapper
  fi

  zle -N $widget $func
}

# Get the keys typed to invoke this widget, as a literal string
function zvm_keys() {
  local keys=${ZVM_KEYS:-$KEYS}

  # Append the prefix of keys if it is visual or visual-line mode
  case "${ZVM_MODE}" in
    $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL)
      if [[ "$keys" != v* ]]; then
        keys="v${keys}"
      fi
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE)
      if [[ "$keys" != V* ]]; then
        keys="V${keys}"
      fi
      ;;
  esac

  # Escape the newline and space characters, otherwise, we can't
  # get the output from subshell correctly.
  keys=${keys//$'\n'/$ZVM_ESCAPE_NEWLINE}
  keys=${keys// /$ZVM_ESCAPE_SPACE}

  # Fix cursor style is not updated in tmux environment, when
  # there are one more panel in the same window, the program
  # in other panel could change the cursor shape, we need to
  # update cursor style when line is redrawing.
  if [[ -n $TMUX ]]; then
    zvm_update_cursor
    # Fix display is not updated in the terminal of IntelliJ IDE.
    [[ "$TERMINAL_EMULATOR" == "JetBrains-JediTerm" ]] && zle redisplay
  fi
  zvm_update_highlight
  zvm_update_repeat_commands
}

# Start every prompt in the correct vi mode
function zvm_zle-line-init() {
  # Save last mode
  local mode=${ZVM_MODE:-$ZVM_MODE_INSERT}

  # It's necessary to set to insert mode when line init
  # and we don't need to reset prompt.
  zvm_select_vi_mode $ZVM_MODE_INSERT false

  # Select line init mode and reset prompt
  case ${ZVM_LINE_INIT_MODE:-$mode} in
    $ZVM_MODE_INSERT) zvm_select_vi_mode $ZVM_MODE_INSERT;;
    *) zvm_select_vi_mode $ZVM_MODE_NORMAL;;
  esac
}

# Restore the user default cursor style after prompt finish
function zvm_zle-line-finish() {
  # When we start a program (e.g. vim, bash, etc.) from the
  # command line, the cursor style is inherited by other
  # programs, so that we need to reset the cursor style to
  # default before executing a command and set the custom
  # style again when the command exits. This way makes any
  # other interactive CLI application would not be affected
  # by it.
  local shape=$(zvm_cursor_style $ZVM_CURSOR_USER_DEFAULT)
  zvm_set_cursor $shape
  zvm_switch_keyword_history=()
}

# Initialize vi-mode for widgets, keybindings, etc.
function zvm_init() {
  # Check if it has been initialized
  if $ZVM_INIT_DONE; then
    return;
  fi

  # Mark plugin initial status
  ZVM_INIT_DONE=true

  zvm_exec_commands 'before_init'

  # Correct the readkey engine
  case $ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE in
    $ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE_NEX|$ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE_ZLE);;
    *)
      echo -n "Warning: Unsupported readkey engine! "
      echo "ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE=$ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE"
      ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE=$ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE_DEFAULT
      ;;
  esac

  # Reduce ESC delay (zle default is 0.4 seconds)
  # Set to 0.01 second delay for taking over the key input processing
  case $ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE in
    $ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE_NEX) KEYTIMEOUT=1;;
    $ZVM_READKEY_ENGINE_ZLE) KEYTIMEOUT=$(($ZVM_KEYTIMEOUT*100));;
  esac

  # Create User-defined widgets

content.content:
function zvm_select_vi_mode() {
  local mode=$1
  local reset_prompt=${2:-true}

  # Check if current mode is the same with the new mode
  if [[ $mode == "$ZVM_MODE" ]]; then
    zvm_update_cursor
    return
  fi

  zvm_exec_commands 'before_select_vi_mode'

  # Some plugins would reset the prompt when we select the
  # keymap, so here we postpone executing reset-prompt.
  zvm_postpone_reset_prompt true

  # Exit operator pending mode
  if $ZVM_OPPEND_MODE; then
    zvm_exit_oppend_mode false
  fi

  case $mode in
    $ZVM_MODE_NORMAL)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_NORMAL
      zvm_update_cursor
      zle vi-cmd-mode
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_INSERT)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_INSERT
      zvm_update_cursor
      zle vi-insert
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_VISUAL
      zvm_update_cursor
      zle visual-mode
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE
      zvm_update_cursor
      zle visual-line-mode
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_REPLACE)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_REPLACE
      zvm_enter_oppend_mode
      ;;
  esac

  # This aspect provides you a moment to do something, such as
  # update the cursor, prompt and so on.
  zvm_exec_commands 'after_select_vi_mode'

  # Stop and trigger reset-prompt
  $reset_prompt && zvm_postpone_reset_prompt false true

  # Start the lazy keybindings when the first time entering the
  # normal mode, when the mode is the same as last mode, we get
  # empty value for $mode.
  if [[ $mode == $ZVM_MODE_NORMAL ]] &&
    (( $#ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS_LIST > 0 )); then

    zvm_exec_commands 'before_lazy_keybindings'

    # Here we should unset the list for normal keybindings
    local list=("${ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS_LIST[@]}")
    unset ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS_LIST

    for r in "${list[@]}"; do
      eval "zvm_bindkey ${r}"
    done

    zvm_exec_commands 'after_lazy_keybindings'
  fi
}

# Postpone reset prompt
function zvm_postpone_reset_prompt() {
  local toggle=$1
  local force=${2:-false}

  if $force; then
    ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=1
  fi

  if $toggle; then
    ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=0
  else
    if (($ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT > 0)); then
      ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=-1
      zle reset-prompt
    else
      ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=-1
    fi
  fi
}

# Reset prompt
function zvm_reset_prompt() {
  if (($ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT >= 0)); then
    ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=$(($ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT + 1))
    return
  fi
  if [[ $ZVM_RESET_PROMPT_DISABLED == true ]]; then
    return
  fi
  local -i retval=0
  if [[ -z "$rawfunc" ]]; then
    zle .reset-prompt -- "$@"
    retval=$?
  else
    $rawfunc -- "$@"
    retval=$?
  fi
  return $retval
}

# Undo action in vi insert mode
#
# CTRL-U  Remove all characters between the cursor position and
#         the beginning of the line.  Previous versions of vim
#         deleted all characters on the line.

      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_VISUAL
      zvm_update_cursor
      zle visual-mode
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE
      zvm_update_cursor
      zle visual-line-mode
      ;;
    $ZVM_MODE_REPLACE)
      ZVM_MODE=$ZVM_MODE_REPLACE
      zvm_enter_oppend_mode
      ;;
  esac

  # This aspect provides you a moment to do something, such as
  # update the cursor, prompt and so on.
  zvm_exec_commands 'after_select_vi_mode'

  # Stop and trigger reset-prompt
  $reset_prompt && zvm_postpone_reset_prompt false true

  # Start the lazy keybindings when the first time entering the
  # normal mode, when the mode is the same as last mode, we get
  # empty value for $mode.
  if [[ $mode == $ZVM_MODE_NORMAL ]] &&
    (( $#ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS_LIST > 0 )); then

    zvm_exec_commands 'before_lazy_keybindings'

    # Here we should unset the list for normal keybindings
    local list=("${ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS_LIST[@]}")
    unset ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS_LIST

    for r in "${list[@]}"; do
      eval "zvm_bindkey ${r}"
    done

    zvm_exec_commands 'after_lazy_keybindings'
  fi
}

# Postpone reset prompt
function zvm_postpone_reset_prompt() {
  local toggle=$1
  local force=${2:-false}

  if $force; then
    ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=1
  fi

  if $toggle; then
    ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=0
  else
    if (($ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT > 0)); then
      ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=-1
      zle reset-prompt
    else
      ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=-1
    fi
  fi
}

# Reset prompt
function zvm_reset_prompt() {
  if (($ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT >= 0)); then
    ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT=$(($ZVM_POSTPONE_RESET_PROMPT + 1))
    return
  fi
  if [[ $ZVM_RESET_PROMPT_DISABLED == true ]]; then
    return
  fi
  local -i retval=0
  if [[ -z "$rawfunc" ]]; then
    zle .reset-prompt -- "$@"
    retval=$?
  else
    $rawfunc -- "$@"
    retval=$?
  fi
  return $retval
}

# Undo action in vi insert mode
#
# CTRL-U  Remove all characters between the cursor position and
#         the beginning of the line.  Previous versions of vim
#         deleted all characters on the line.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env zsh
# Drives an interactive zsh (omp transient + zsh-vi-mode) through zpty:
# run a command, type text, send Ctrl+C, probe ZVM state and vi-mode behavior.
zmodload zsh/zpty || exit 1

export TERM=xterm-256color
export ZDOTDIR=/tmp/omp-test/zdot
STATE=/tmp/omp-test/state.log
rm -f $STATE /tmp/omp-test/typescript

zpty omp script -qfec 'zsh -i' /tmp/omp-test/typescript

send() { zpty -w -n omp "$1"; }

sleep 3 # first prompt + lazy zvm init

# a normal command cycle
send $'true\n'
sleep 1

# state before interrupt
send $'{ print -r -- "S1 mode=$ZVM_MODE insert=$ZVM_INSERT_MODE widget=${widgets[zle-line-init]}"; bindkey -lL main } >> /tmp/omp-test/state.log\n'
sleep 1

# type a command, do not run it, then Ctrl+C
send 'ls -l'
sleep 1
send $'\x03'
sleep 1

# state after interrupt
send $'{ print -r -- "S2 mode=$ZVM_MODE insert=$ZVM_INSERT_MODE"; bindkey -lL main } >> /tmp/omp-test/state.log\n'
sleep 1

# functional probe: ESC should enter normal mode, then i re-enters insert.
# working vi-mode runs `echo PROBE_OK`; broken vi-mode runs `iecho PROBE_OK`.
send $'\x1b'
sleep 1
send $'iecho PROBE_OK\n'
sleep 1

send $'{ print -r -- "S3 mode=$ZVM_MODE insert=$ZVM_INSERT_MODE" } >> /tmp/omp-test/state.log\n'
sleep 1

send $'exit\n'
sleep 1
zpty -d omp 2>/dev/null

print -- "=== state.log ==="
cat $STATE
print -- "=== typescript (tail, escapes stripped) ==="
sed -e 's/\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[a-zA-Z]//g' -e 's/\x1b\][^\x07]*\x07//g' -e 's/\x0d//g' /tmp/omp-test/typescript | grep -v '^$' | tail -25

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env zsh
# Drives an interactive zsh (omp transient + zsh-vi-mode) through zpty:
# run a command, type text, send Ctrl+C, probe ZVM state around it.
zmodload zsh/zpty || exit 1

D=$HOME/omp-test
export TERM=xterm-256color
export ZDOTDIR=$D/zdot
STATE=$D/state.log
rm -f $STATE

zpty omp zsh -i

send() { zpty -w -n omp "$1"; }

sleep 3 # first prompt + lazy zvm init

# a normal command cycle
send $'true\n'
sleep 1

# state before interrupt

#!/usr/bin/env zsh
# Drives an interactive zsh (omp transient + zsh-vi-mode) through zpty:
# run a command, type text, send Ctrl+C, probe ZVM state and vi-mode behavior.
zmodload zsh/zpty || exit 1

export TERM=xterm-256color
export ZDOTDIR=/tmp/omp-test/zdot
STATE=/tmp/omp-test/state.log
rm -f $STATE /tmp/omp-test/typescript

zpty omp script -qfec 'zsh -i' /tmp/omp-test/typescript

send() { zpty -w -n omp "$1"; }

sleep 3 # first prompt + lazy zvm init

# a normal command cycle
send $'true\n'
sleep 1

# state before interrupt
send $'{ print -r -- "S1 mode=$ZVM_MODE insert=$ZVM_INSERT_MODE widget=${widgets[zle-line-init]}"; bindkey -lL main } >> /tmp/omp-test/state.log\n'
sleep 1

# type a command, do not run it, then Ctrl+C
send 'ls -l'
sleep 1
send $'\x03'
sleep 1

# state after interrupt
send $'{ print -r -- "S2 mode=$ZVM_MODE insert=$ZVM_INSERT_MODE"; bindkey -lL main } >> /tmp/omp-test/state.log\n'
sleep 1

# functional probe: ESC should enter normal mode, then i re-enters insert.
# working vi-mode runs `echo PROBE_OK`; broken vi-mode runs `iecho PROBE_OK`.
send $'\x1b'
sleep 1
send $'iecho PROBE_OK\n'
sleep 1

send $'{ print -r -- "S3 mode=$ZVM_MODE insert=$ZVM_INSERT_MODE" } >> /tmp/omp-test/state.log\n'
sleep 1

send $'exit\n'
sleep 1
zpty -d omp 2>/dev/null

print -- "=== state.log ==="
cat $STATE
print -- "=== typescript (tail, escapes stripped) ==="
sed -e 's/\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[a-zA-Z]//g' -e 's/\x1b\][^\x07]*\x07//g' -e 's/\x0d//g' /tmp/omp-test/typescript | grep -v '^$' | tail -25

# Paste content from system clipboard after cursor
function zvm_paste_clipboard_after() {
  local content=$(zvm_clipboard_get)
  [[ -z $content ]] && return
  local saved=$CUTBUFFER
  CUTBUFFER=$content
  zvm_vi_put_after
  CUTBUFFER=$saved
}

# Paste content from system clipboard before cursor
function zvm_paste_clipboard_before() {
  local content=$(zvm_clipboard_get)
  [[ -z $content ]] && return
  local saved=$CUTBUFFER
  CUTBUFFER=$content
  zvm_vi_put_before
  CUTBUFFER=$saved
}

# Paste content from system clipboard in visual mode
function zvm_visual_paste_clipboard() {
  local content=$(zvm_clipboard_get)
  if [[ -z $content ]]; then
    zvm_exit_visual_mode
    return
  fi
  local ret=($(zvm_calc_selection))
  local bpos=$ret[1] epos=$ret[2]
  local cpos=$((bpos + $#content - 1))
  CUTBUFFER=${BUFFER:$bpos:$((epos-bpos))}
  BUFFER="${BUFFER:0:$bpos}${content}${BUFFER:$epos}"
  CURSOR=$cpos
  zvm_exit_visual_mode
}

# Updates editor information when line pre redraw
function zvm_zle-line-pre-redraw() {
  # Fix cursor style is not updated in tmux environment, when
  # there are one more panel in the same window, the program
  # in other panel could change the cursor shape, we need to
  # update cursor style when line is redrawing.
  if [[ -n $TMUX ]]; then
    zvm_update_cursor
    # Fix display is not updated in the terminal of IntelliJ IDE.
    [[ "$TERMINAL_EMULATOR" == "JetBrains-JediTerm" ]] && zle redisplay
  fi
  zvm_update_highlight
  zvm_update_repeat_commands
}

#!/usr/bin/env zsh
# Scenario: accept a line from NORMAL mode via Enter (no Ctrl+C), then probe
# whether vi-mode still works on the next line.
zmodload zsh/zpty || exit 1

D=$HOME/omp-test
export TERM=xterm-256color
export ZDOTDIR=$D/zdot
STATE=$D/state2.log
rm -f $STATE

zpty omp zsh -i
send() { zpty -w -n omp "$1"; }

sleep 3

send $'true\n'
sleep 1
send "{ print -r -- \"S1 mode=\$ZVM_MODE insert=\$ZVM_INSERT_MODE\"; bindkey -lL main } >> $STATE"$'\n'
sleep 1

# type a command, ESC to normal mode, accept with Enter from vicmd
send 'echo dummy'
sleep 1
send $'\x1b'
sleep 1
send $'\n'
sleep 1

send "{ print -r -- \"S2-enter-from-normal mode=\$ZVM_MODE insert=\$ZVM_INSERT_MODE\"; bindkey -lL main } >> $STATE"$'\n'
sleep 1

# functional probe: ESC, i, then a command. Broken vi-mode leaves stray chars.
send $'\x1b'
sleep 1
send 'i'
sleep 1
send " true; { print -r -- \"S3-probe status_before=\$? mode=\$ZVM_MODE insert=\$ZVM_INSERT_MODE\" } >> $STATE"$'\n'
sleep 1

send $'exit\n'
sleep 1
zpty -d omp 2>/dev/null

print -- "=== state2.log ==="
cat $STATE

#!/usr/bin/env zsh
# Mode-aware scenario driver. Usage: driver3.zsh <zdotdir> <statefile>
# ZVM default: each line starts in the mode the previous line ended in.
zmodload zsh/zpty || exit 1

D=$HOME/omp-test
export TERM=xterm-256color
export ZDOTDIR=$1
STATE=$2
rm -f $STATE

zpty omp zsh -i
send() { zpty -w -n omp "$1"; }
dump() { send "{ print -r -- \"$1 last_status=\$? mode=\$ZVM_MODE keymap_main=\$(bindkey -lL main)\" } >> $STATE"$'\n'; sleep 1; }

sleep 3

send $'true\n'; sleep 1
# line starts in insert mode
dump S1-initial

# insert start expected: type, ESC to normal, accept from normal mode
send 'echo dummy'; sleep 1
send $'\x1b'; sleep 1
send $'\n'; sleep 1

# normal start expected: i enters insert, then dump
send 'i'; sleep 1
dump S2-after-normal-accept

# insert start expected (last mode i): type, ESC, then Ctrl+C from normal mode
send 'ls -x'; sleep 1
send $'\x1b'; sleep 1
send $'\x03'; sleep 1

# normal start expected (last mode n): i enters insert, then dump
send 'i'; sleep 1
dump S3-after-normal-ctrlc

# insert start expected: plain dump proves typing works
dump S4-final

send $'exit\n'; sleep 1
zpty -d omp 2>/dev/null

cat $STATE

content.content:
function zvm_default_handler() {
  local keys=$(zvm_keys)
  local extra_keys=$1

  # Exit vi mode if keys is the escape keys
  case $(zvm_escape_non_printed_characters "$keys") in
    '^['|$ZVM_VI_INSERT_ESCAPE_BINDKEY)
      zvm_exit_insert_mode false
      zvm_reset_prompt
      ZVM_KEYS=${extra_keys}
      return
      ;;
    [vV]'^['|[vV]$ZVM_VI_VISUAL_ESCAPE_BINDKEY)
      zvm_exit_visual_mode false
      zvm_reset_prompt
      ZVM_KEYS=${extra_keys}
      return
      ;;
  esac

  case "$KEYMAP" in
    vicmd)
      case "$keys" in
        [vV]c) zvm_vi_change false;;
        [vV]d) zvm_vi_delete false;;
        [vV]y) zvm_vi_yank false;;
        [vV]S) zvm_change_surround S;;
        [cdyvV]*)
          # We must loop util we meet a valid range action
          while :; do
            zvm_range_handler "${keys}${extra_keys}"
            case $? in
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_OK)
                # The range action is handled successfully and exit
                break
                ;;
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_CONTINUE)
                # Continue to ask to provide the action when we're
                # still in visual mode
                keys='v'; extra_keys=
                ;;
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_PUSHBACK)
                # Push the keys onto the input stack of ZLE, it's
                # handled in zvm_readkeys_handler function
                zvm_exit_visual_mode false
                zvm_reset_prompt
                return
                ;;
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_CANCEL)
                # Exit visual mode and cancel the range action
                zvm_exit_visual_mode false
                zvm_reset_prompt
                break
                ;;
            esac
          done
          ;;
        *)
          local i=
          for ((i=0;i<$#keys;i++)) do
            zvm_navigation_handler ${keys:$i:1}
            zvm_highlight
          done
          ;;
      esac
      ;;
    viins|main)
      if [[ "${keys:0:1}" =~ [a-zA-Z0-9\ ] ]]; then
        zvm_self_insert "${keys:0:1}"
        zle redisplay
        ZVM_KEYS="${keys:1}${extra_keys}"
        return
      elif [[ "${keys:0:1}" == $'\e' ]]; then
        zvm_exit_insert_mode false
        ZVM_KEYS="${keys:1}${extra_keys}"
        return
      fi
      ;;
    visual)
      ;;
  esac

  ZVM_KEYS=
}

# Read keys for retrieving and executing a widget
function zvm_readkeys_handler() {
  local keymap=${1}
  local keys=${2:-$KEYS}
  local key=
  local widget=

  # Get the keymap if keymap is empty
  if [[ -z $keymap ]]; then
    case "$ZVM_MODE" in
      $ZVM_MODE_INSERT) keymap=viins;;
      $ZVM_MODE_NORMAL) keymap=vicmd;;
      $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL|$ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE) keymap=visual;;
    esac
  fi

  # Read keys and retrieve the widget
  zvm_readkeys $keymap $keys
  keys=${retval[1]}
  widget=${retval[2]}
  key=${retval[3]}

  # Escape space in keys
  keys=${keys//$ZVM_ESCAPE_SPACE/ }
  key=${key//$ZVM_ESCAPE_SPACE/ }

  ZVM_KEYS="${keys}"

  # If the widget is current handler, we should call the default handler
  if [[ "${widget}" == "${funcstack[1]}" ]]; then
    widget=
  fi

  # If the widget isn't matched, we should call the default handler
  if [[ -z ${widget} ]]; then
    # Disable reset prompt action, as multiple calling this function
    # will cause potential line eaten issue.
    ZVM_RESET_PROMPT_DISABLED=true

    zle zvm_default_handler "$key"

    ZVM_RESET_PROMPT_DISABLED=false

    # Push back to the key input stack, and postpone reset prompt
    if [[ -n "$ZVM_KEYS" ]]; then
      # To prevent ZLE from error "not enough arguments for -U", the
      # parameter should be put after `--` symbols.
      zle -U -- "${ZVM_KEYS}"
    else
      # If there is any reset prompt, we need to execute for
      # prompt resetting.
      zvm_postpone_reset_prompt false
    fi
  else
    zle $widget
  fi

  ZVM_KEYS=
}

# Find and move cursor to next character
function zvm_find_and_move_cursor() {
  local char=$1
  local count=${2:-1}
  local forward=${3:-true}
  local skip=${4:-false}
  local cursor=$CURSOR

function zvm_default_handler() {
  local keys=$(zvm_keys)
  local extra_keys=$1

  # Exit vi mode if keys is the escape keys
  case $(zvm_escape_non_printed_characters "$keys") in
    '^['|$ZVM_VI_INSERT_ESCAPE_BINDKEY)
      zvm_exit_insert_mode false
      zvm_reset_prompt
      ZVM_KEYS=${extra_keys}
      return
      ;;
    [vV]'^['|[vV]$ZVM_VI_VISUAL_ESCAPE_BINDKEY)
      zvm_exit_visual_mode false
      zvm_reset_prompt
      ZVM_KEYS=${extra_keys}
      return
      ;;
  esac

  case "$KEYMAP" in
    vicmd)
      case "$keys" in
        [vV]c) zvm_vi_change false;;
        [vV]d) zvm_vi_delete false;;
        [vV]y) zvm_vi_yank false;;
        [vV]S) zvm_change_surround S;;
        [cdyvV]*)
          # We must loop util we meet a valid range action
          while :; do
            zvm_range_handler "${keys}${extra_keys}"
            case $? in
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_OK)
                # The range action is handled successfully and exit
                break
                ;;
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_CONTINUE)
                # Continue to ask to provide the action when we're
                # still in visual mode
                keys='v'; extra_keys=
                ;;
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_PUSHBACK)
                # Push the keys onto the input stack of ZLE, it's
                # handled in zvm_readkeys_handler function
                zvm_exit_visual_mode false
                zvm_reset_prompt
                return
                ;;
              $ZVM_RANGE_HANDLER_RET_CANCEL)
                # Exit visual mode and cancel the range action
                zvm_exit_visual_mode false
                zvm_reset_prompt
                break
                ;;
            esac
          done
          ;;
        *)
          local i=
          for ((i=0;i<$#keys;i++)) do
            zvm_navigation_handler ${keys:$i:1}
            zvm_highlight
          done
          ;;
      esac
      ;;
    viins|main)
      if [[ "${keys:0:1}" =~ [a-zA-Z0-9\ ] ]]; then
        zvm_self_insert "${keys:0:1}"
        zle redisplay
        ZVM_KEYS="${keys:1}${extra_keys}"
        return
      elif [[ "${keys:0:1}" == $'\e' ]]; then
        zvm_exit_insert_mode false
        ZVM_KEYS="${keys:1}${extra_keys}"
        return
      fi
      ;;
    visual)
      ;;
  esac

  ZVM_KEYS=
}

# Read keys for retrieving and executing a widget
function zvm_readkeys_handler() {
  local keymap=${1}
  local keys=${2:-$KEYS}
  local key=
  local widget=

  # Get the keymap if keymap is empty
  if [[ -z $keymap ]]; then
    case "$ZVM_MODE" in
      $ZVM_MODE_INSERT) keymap=viins;;
      $ZVM_MODE_NORMAL) keymap=vicmd;;
      $ZVM_MODE_VISUAL|$ZVM_MODE_VISUAL_LINE) keymap=visual;;
    esac
  fi

  # Read keys and retrieve the widget
  zvm_readkeys $keymap $keys
  keys=${retval[1]}
  widget=${retval[2]}
  key=${retval[3]}

  # Escape space in keys
  keys=${keys//$ZVM_ESCAPE_SPACE/ }
  key=${key//$ZVM_ESCAPE_SPACE/ }

    ZVM_RESET_PROMPT_DISABLED=true

      # prompt resetting.
      zvm_postpone_reset_prompt false
    fi
  else
    zle $widget
  fi

  ZVM_KEYS=
}

#!/usr/bin/env zsh
# Verifies the transient prompt renders: Enter path and Ctrl+C path.
# Usage: driver4.zsh <zdotdir>
zmodload zsh/zpty || exit 1

export TERM=xterm-256color
export ZDOTDIR=$1

zpty omp zsh -i
send() { zpty -w -n omp "$1"; }

sleep 3
send $'echo enter-path\n'
sleep 1
send 'typed-then-interrupted'
sleep 1
send $'\x03'
sleep 1
send $'echo after-interrupt\n'
sleep 1
send $'exit\n'
sleep 1

local out= chunk=
while zpty -r omp chunk 2>/dev/null; do out+=$chunk; done
zpty -d omp 2>/dev/null

print -- "TTT_count=${#${(M)${(f)out}:#*TTT>*}}"
print -rn -- $out | sed -e $'s/\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[a-zA-Z]//g' -e $'s/\x1b\][^\x07]*\x07//g' -e $'s/\x0d//g' | grep -v '^$'

content.text:
Everything checks out — the transient prompt (`TTT>`) renders on both the Enter and Ctrl+C paths, with and without ZVM, and vi-mode behavior with the fix is identical to native ZVM. Analysis complete; here's the report.

---

## Analysis: [#5992](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/5992) — zsh-vi-mode breaks after Ctrl+C with transient prompt

### Reproduction

Reproduced in WSL (zsh 5.9, zpty-driven interactive session, binary built from `next`): with the transient prompt enabled and zsh-vi-mode (ZVM) loaded, vi-mode dies permanently after accepting **or** interrupting a line from normal mode. The session state then matches the reporter's `declare` diff exactly: `ZVM_MODE=n`, `ZVM_INSERT_MODE=''`, keymap stuck on `viins` — ESC never switches to normal mode again, which reads as "emacs mode". Ctrl+C from insert mode is actually harmless; the trigger is being in **normal mode** when the line ends (vi users hit ESC constantly, and the issue title blames Ctrl+C because that's when they notice).

### Root cause (two layered problems in [omp.zsh](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh))

1. **Ordering inversion.** ZVM initializes lazily at first `precmd`, so it always wraps OMP's `zle-line-init` widget: its wrapper calls `_omp_zle-line-init` first, then `zvm_zle-line-init`. But `_omp_zle-line-init` ([omp.zsh:547](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:547)) runs `zle .recursive-edit`, which consumes the *entire* editing session. ZVM's line-init — the code that realigns the keymap with its `ZVM_MODE` bookkeeping at line start — therefore effectively runs at line *end*. After a normal-mode accept/interrupt, the next line starts with keymap `main` (viins) while `ZVM_MODE` still says `n`. Since `zvm_select_vi_mode` early-returns when asked for the mode it thinks it's already in, ESC → normal becomes a permanent no-op.

2. **A re-entrancy landmine.** ZVM's `zvm_reset_prompt` resolves `$rawfunc` via dynamic scoping (expecting its own widget wrapper's local). When ZVM code runs `zle reset-prompt` from inside OMP's line-init, `$rawfunc` instead resolves to `_omp_decorated_zle-line-init` from the line-init wrapper still on the stack — so ZVM's prompt reset **recursively re-enters OMP's recursive-edit**. I hit this while validating the fix; it silently lurks for any ZVM action during editing today.

### Proposed change (validated end-to-end)

Two lines at the top of `_omp_zle-line-init`, after the `$CONTEXT` check:

```zsh
  # zsh-vi-mode's zle-line-init only runs after ours, i.e. after the line has
  # already been edited; align its keymap/mode bookkeeping up front (#5992).
  # The empty rawfunc shadows zvm_widget_wrapper's local so zvm_reset_prompt
  # can't resolve it dynamically and re-enter this widget.
  local rawfunc=
  [[ $ZVM_INIT_DONE == true ]] && (( $+functions[zvm_zle-line-init] )) && zvm_zle-line-init
```

Verified against a control run (same setup, no transient prompt = native ZVM): insert/normal-mode accepts, normal-mode Ctrl+C, and follow-up mode switches now behave identically to native ZVM, and the transient prompt still renders on every path, with and without ZVM loaded. ZVM's wrapper still calls `zvm_zle-line-init` a second time at line end; that's idempotent and harmless.

### Out of scope

- `$?` after Ctrl+C is `1` instead of `130` — pre-existing `zle .send-break` limitation, already a `TODO` at [omp.zsh:585](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:585); unchanged by this fix (control shows native 130).
- The fish vi-mode transient issue ([#3625](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/3625)) is a different mechanism.

The change is a small edit to `src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh` (embedded at build time), no Go changes, no docs impact. Want me to implement it? Given it's a two-line script change I'd handle it directly and re-run the WSL verification matrix against the rebuilt binary before committing.

1	# Phase 2 — Plan
2	
3	Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
4	must be able to execute each task without asking questions.
5	
6	## Pin the spec
7	
8	Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:
9	
10	- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
11	  them.
12	- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
13	- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
14	  compatibility requirements.
15	- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
16	- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.
17	
18	## Split into tasks
19	
20	- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
21	- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
22	  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
23	  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
24	- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.
25	
26	## Decide the workspace per task
27	
28	- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
29	  and commit the result in the current session.

file.content:
# Phase 2 — Plan

Turn the approved analysis into an executable plan. The quality bar: an implementer-tier model
must be able to execute each task without asking questions.

## Pin the spec

Write the spec down before delegating anything. It contains:

- The decided approach — including decisions already made, so the implementer does not relitigate
  them.
- Files to touch, and the entry points to start from.
- Constraints: style rules, patterns to follow (point at existing code), performance or
  compatibility requirements.
- Verification commands the implementer must run locally (build, tests, lint).
- Explicit non-goals: what the task must NOT change. This is what keeps subagents from wandering.

## Split into tasks

- One task = one self-contained unit an implementer can finish and verify on its own.
- Mark which tasks are independent and which consume another task's output. Parallelize the
  independent ones; sequence the rest. When in doubt, sequence — a merge conflict between two
  parallel subagents costs more than the parallelism saves.
- Documentation updates belong to the task that changes the behavior, not to a separate task.

## Decide the workspace per task

- Main working tree: when the task depends on uncommitted local changes, or when you will review
  and commit the result in the current session.
- Isolated worktree: everything else, especially parallel tasks — they must never share a
  working tree.

## Output of this phase

A task list where each entry names its executor tier (see Phase 3), its workspace, its
dependencies, and carries its pinned spec.
